content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
September ii, 1992
Judith Allen
Public Utilities Counsel
Public Utilities Commission
505 Van Ness Avenue
San Francisco,
CA
94102—3298
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-92—364f
Dear Ms. Allen:
I
am writing to confirm our telephone conversation on
August 28,
1992,
wherein we discussed your request for advice,
Letter No.
A-92—364f,
which requested further clarification of the
advice you received in Letter No.
A—92--364.
I confirmed our
previous advice
and informed you that we do not interpret In re
Stone
(1977)
3
FPPC 52, to apply to the facts provided regarding
Mr.
Timothy Kenney and Mr.
Mark Bumgardner.
This is because these
individuals are designated employees of the
CPUC who are traveling
to Canada to perform audits and inspections which are part of the
regulatory process of the
CPUC as mandated by Public Utilities
Code Section 314(b).
As stated in our prior letter,
the CPUC’s reimbursement of
the travel expenses in question is neither a gift nor income to
its employees,
Mr.
Kenney and Mr.
Bumgardner.
Furthermore,
according to the facts you present,
PG&E’s reimbursement to the
CPUC for the travel expenses of its employees is not a gift to the
employees who will be traveling to Canada to conduct the
inspection.
I trust this answers your question.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
By:
Jil’. Stdther
Coifise1,
Legal Division
SH:JS:aa
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660

sections.question_synthetic: Whether the reimbursement of travel expenses to Canada by CPUC designated employees Timothy Kenney and Mark Bumgardner to perform regulatory audits and inspections constitutes a gift or income under FPPC regulations, and whether PG&E's reimbursement to the CPUC for these expenses constitutes a gift to the employees.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC confirmed that In re Stone does not apply to these facts. The CPUC's reimbursement of travel expenses is neither a gift nor income to the employees, and PG&E's reimbursement to the CPUC for the employees' travel expenses is not a gift to the employees conducting the inspection.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a follow-up confirmation letter to prior advice (A-92-364). No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present; advice is conveyed in letter format. Telephone conversation confirmation documented. Contains specific employee names and regulatory context.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the reimbursement of travel expenses to Canada by CPUC designated employees Timothy Kenney and Mark Bumgardner to perform regulatory audits and inspections constitutes a gift or income under FPPC regulations, and whether PG&E's reimbursement to the CPUC for these expenses constitutes a gift to the employees.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC confirmed that In re Stone does not apply to these facts. The CPUC's reimbursement of travel expenses is neither a gift nor income to the employees, and PG&E's reimbursement to the CPUC for the employees' travel expenses is not a gift to the employees conducting the inspection.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission September ii, 1992 Judith Allen Public Utilities Counsel Public Utilities Commission 505 Van Ness Avenue San Francisco, CA 94102—3298 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-92—364f Dear Ms. Allen: I am writing to confirm our telephone conversation on August 28, 1992, wherein we discussed your request for advice, Letter No. A-92—364f, which requested further clarification of the advice you received in Letter No. A—92--364. I confirmed our previous advice and informed you that we do not interpret In re Stone (1977) 3 FPPC 52, to apply to the facts provided regarding Mr. Timothy Kenney and Mr. Mark Bumgardner. This is because these individuals are designated employees of the CPUC who are traveling to Canada to perform audits and inspections which are part of the regulatory process of the CPUC as mandated by Public Utilities Code Section 314(b). As stated in our prior letter, the CPUC’s reimbursement of the travel expenses in question is neither a gift nor income to its employees, Mr. Kenney and Mr. Bumgardner. Furthermore, according to the facts you present, PG&E’s reimbursement to the CPUC for the travel expenses of its employees is not a gift to the employees who will be traveling to Canada to conduct the inspection. I trust this answers your question. Sincerely, Scott Hallabrin Acting General Counsel By: Jil’. Stdther Coifise1, Legal Division SH:JS:aa 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660
embedding.summary: FPPC confirms previous advice regarding travel expense reimbursement for CPUC designated employees conducting regulatory audits in Canada, clarifying that such reimbursements do not constitute gifts or income under applicable law.
